description:
You are on–call as an operations engineer.  An alert has reported that
    several command-line utilities on this host are out–of-date and might be the
    reason for a series of “segmentation fault” incidents.  
    Your job is to triage the incident by verifying the versions that are
    currently installed, attempting to upgrade the affected packages in
    parallel, and then writing a **single, strictly-formatted log file** that
    our post-mortem tooling can ingest automatically.

    Packages involved  
        1. curl  
        2. grep  
        3. nano  

    Work-flow overview
        1.  Create the directory /home/user/incident_logs if it does not
            already exist.  (No elevated privileges are available, so stay in
            $HOME.)
        2.  For each of the three packages, determine the currently installed
            version using any package-management command you prefer
            (e.g. “dpkg -s”, “apt list --installed”, etc.).
        3.  Attempt to upgrade all three packages **concurrently**.  Fire the
            upgrade commands off in the background (&) and use “wait” so that
            the script does not finish until all three have completed.  
            •  If an upgrade succeeds, capture the new version.  
            •  If the upgrade cannot be performed without root, record the
               pre-existing version for both “BEFORE_VERSION” and
               “AFTER_VERSION” but still mark the operation as “SUCCESS” (this
               is a purely informational incident triage).
        4.  Produce the summary log /home/user/incident_logs/package_triage.log.
            The file **must** contain exactly four lines and nothing else.
            Lines **1–3** must be listed in **alphabetical order by package
            name** (curl, grep, nano).  
            Line **4** is a fixed trailer that shows the number of operations.

            Required line format (pipe-delimited, no surrounding spaces):

              YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS|UPDATE|<PACKAGE>|<BEFORE_VERSION>|<AFTER_VERSION>|SUCCESS

            Example (do **NOT** hard-code these values):

              2024-03-29T14:22:31|UPDATE|curl|7.68.0-1ubuntu2.18|7.68.0-1ubuntu2.19|SUCCESS
              2024-03-29T14:22:31|UPDATE|grep|3.6-1build1|3.6-1build1|SUCCESS
              2024-03-29T14:22:31|UPDATE|nano|5.4-2ubuntu2|5.4-2ubuntu2|SUCCESS
              END|TRIAGE_COMPLETE|3

            ❶  Timestamp must be in ISO-8601 form (to-the-second precision, no
               timezone suffix).  
            ❷  The literal string “UPDATE” must appear as shown.  
            ❸  The literal string “SUCCESS” must appear as shown.  
            ❹  The trailer line **must** read exactly:
                    END|TRIAGE_COMPLETE|3

    Important constraints
        •  Do not create additional files inside /home/user/incident_logs.  
        •  Do not include blank lines or comments in package_triage.log.  
        •  Work entirely as the normal user; root escalation (sudo, su) is not
           possible inside the execution environment.  
        •  Make sure you use background operators (&) so that at least two of
           the three upgrade commands are running in parallel before you issue
           “wait”.

    The automated grader will verify:
        •  /home/user/incident_logs exists.  
        •  /home/user/incident_logs/package_triage.log exists, is a regular
           file, and contains exactly four newline-terminated records.  
        •  Lines 1–3 are for curl, grep, nano in that order and conform to the
           pipe-delimited schema above.  
        •  Line 4 matches the fixed trailer text verbatim.

    Once the correctly-formatted log file is present, the incident will be
    considered triaged.
truth:
Required artefacts after the agent finishes:

    1. Directory:
       Path: /home/user/incident_logs
       •  Must exist and be accessible (rwx------ or rwxr-xr-x are both OK).

    2. File:
       Path: /home/user/incident_logs/package_triage.log

       Absolute verification rules:
         a. The file must contain exactly 4 lines (4 newline characters).
         b. Line 1 begins with an ISO-8601 timestamp, contains “|UPDATE|curl|”,
            and ends with “|SUCCESS”.
         c. Line 2 begins with an ISO-8601 timestamp, contains “|UPDATE|grep|”,
            and ends with “|SUCCESS”.
         d. Line 3 begins with an ISO-8601 timestamp, contains “|UPDATE|nano|”,
            and ends with “|SUCCESS”.
         e. Line 4 is exactly:
                END|TRIAGE_COMPLETE|3

    The grader will not attempt to validate the specific version numbers; it
    will only ensure that non-empty strings occupy the BEFORE_VERSION and
    AFTER_VERSION fields (field positions 4 and 5).

    Note: A line passes the timestamp test if it matches the regexp  
          ^20[0-9]{2}-[01][0-9]-[0-3][0-9]T[0-2][0-9]:[0-5][0-9]:[0-5][0-9]\|
